name: Road cut on E side of US 87
short_description: Roadside outcrop along US 87 exposing Paleozoic limestone and chert formations typical of the Llano Uplift. Easy-access site for collecting agate, chalcedony, and quartz specimens.
description:
This roadside cut on the east side of US 87 near Johnson City in Gillespie County offers convenient access to geological formations characteristic of the Llano Uplift, a 1.3-billion-year-old basement rock complex that brings Precambrian and Paleozoic rocks to the surface. The exposed road cut displays limestone and chert layers from the Paleozoic era, providing excellent opportunities for finding agate nodules, chalcedony, and quartz fragments weathered from the bedrock.

The site benefits from natural erosion and road maintenance that continuously exposes fresh material. Collectors can examine the outcrop face directly from the roadside without extensive hiking, making this an accessible spot for families and rockhounders of all experience levels. The road cut geology reveals the complex mineralization processes that occurred when silica-rich fluids percolated through carbonate rocks during multiple geological episodes.

Best collecting involves scanning the base of the outcrop where weathered material accumulates and examining loose fragments on the ground surface. The Gillespie County area is part of the prolific Llano region known for producing quality agate and chalcedony specimens that attract rockhounds from across Texas. This location provides a quick roadside stop opportunity without requiring special access permissions or extensive driving on backcountry roads.

Expect to find primarily small to medium-sized specimens, with occasional larger nodules. The material here represents natural weathering rather than fresh excavation, so careful observation of color variations in the rocks is essential for identifying the best collectible specimens.
history: This road cut exposes rocks from the Llano Uplift, an ancient Precambrian basement complex that thrusts Paleozoic limestone, dolomite, and chert formations to the surface across central Texas. The chert and agate deposits here formed when silica-rich hydrothermal fluids replaced carbonate host rocks during multiple mineralizing events spanning 300+ million years, creating the nodular and vein deposits that collectors seek today.
tips: Scan the base of the road cut and the ground surface on both sides of US 87, where weathered nodules naturally weather out and collect. Use a small hand lens or magnifying glass to identify agate banding and chalcedony coloration in fragments before collecting. Look for specimens with translucent qualities, distinctive color banding in whites, tans, and grays, and the characteristic waxy luster typical of chalcedony varieties from this region. Bring a bucket or bag since material here tends to be scattered rather than concentrated in one area.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 1903 feet in the Texas Hill Country. The site is directly adjacent to US 87 on a roadside outcrop with minimal elevation change. Terrain is rocky with scattered brush vegetation typical of the Llano region. Ground surface is uneven with exposed bedrock fragments and loose material. Walking surface is relatively flat along the roadside but includes scattered rocks and broken chert that require careful footing. No significant hiking required - parking and collecting occur within 50 feet of the road.
best_season: October - April. Spring wildflower season (March-April) can make the area more attractive, and cooler fall and winter months reduce heat exposure. Summer temperatures in central Texas often exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit, making roadside collecting uncomfortable during midday hours.
written_directions: From Johnson City, take US 87 north toward Llano. The road cut is located on the east side of US 87 approximately 3-4 miles north of Johnson City. Look for the outcrop exposure on your right as you drive north. Pullouts exist on both sides of the highway near the exposure. GPS coordinates 30.161106, -98.893423 will place you directly at the site. The highway is paved and well-maintained. Park safely off the roadway and scan the exposed rock face and surrounding ground surface.
road_conditions: US 87 is a paved state highway in good condition. No special clearance needed. Pullouts are unpaved but suitable for standard passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts on both east and west sides of US 87 near the road cut. Limited parking - space for 2-3 vehicles. No facilities or amenities. High-speed traffic on US 87 requires caution when exiting vehicles.
collection_rules: Personal collection of reasonable amounts of naturally weathered material from the roadside is typically permitted on this public road corridor. Collectors should not excavate, hammer into the road cut face, or extract material that requires tools or significant disturbance. Do not trespass on adjacent private property. Follow all Texas Department of Transportation regulations regarding roadside collection. Leave the site in the condition you found it.
rules:
Collect only naturally weathered material from ground surface
No excavation or hammering of the road cut face
No trespassing on adjacent private land
Obey traffic laws and park safely off the roadway
Do not remove specimens larger than can be carried by hand
Leave the site undisturbed for future collectors
nearest_services: Johnson City, approximately 3-4 miles south. Limited services including a small general store. Nearest full services (fuel, food, lodging) in Llano, approximately 15 miles north, or Blanco, approximately 10 miles east.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat roadside terrain with minimal elevation change and no significant hiking required. Uneven rocky ground surface with scattered chert fragments requires sturdy footwear but is accessible to most mobility levels. Parking immediately adjacent to collecting area requires careful vehicle positioning near active highway. Road cut face is visible from the roadside without climbing or scrambling needed.

faq.question: What types of agates and gemstones can you find at the US 87 road cut near Johnson City?
faq.answer: This Gillespie County roadside exposure yields agate nodules, chalcedony fragments, and quartz specimens weathered from Paleozoic limestone and chert formations. Specimens typically display white, tan, gray, and occasional pink coloration with the characteristic waxy luster of chalcedony varieties. The site is part of the prolific Llano Uplift region known for quality agate and chert material.

faq.question: How much does it cost to rockhound at the US 87 road cut, and do you need a permit?
faq.answer: No permit is required and there is no fee to collect naturally weathered material from this public roadside location. Simply pull off safely along US 87 on the east side and collect from the ground surface. Commercial collection is not permitted, and collection is limited to personal use quantities of naturally weathered specimens.

faq.question: Is the US 87 road cut suitable for beginner rockhounds and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner-friendly site. No special skills, permits, or equipment are required. Children as young as 6 can participate in identifying and collecting specimens from the ground surface. The location offers immediate roadside access without extensive hiking, making it ideal for families and newcomers to rockhounding.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at this Gillespie County site?
faq.answer: October through April is optimal for collecting at the US 87 road cut. These months offer cooler temperatures, avoiding the intense Texas Hill Country summer heat. Spring (March-April) combines pleasant weather with wildflower displays in the surrounding area. Avoid summer months when temperatures frequently exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit.

faq.question: What tools and equipment should you bring to collect at the US 87 road cut near Johnson City?
faq.answer: Minimal equipment is needed at this site since you are collecting naturally weathered surface material. Bring a collection bag or bucket, sturdy walking shoes for rocky terrain, a small hand lens or magnifying glass to identify agate banding and chalcedony characteristics, and water. Sunscreen and a hat are recommended. No hammers or digging tools should be used on the road cut face.

faq.question: How do you find the best agate specimens at this Llano Uplift roadside location?
faq.answer: Focus your search on the base of the road cut where weathered material naturally concentrates, and scan the ground surface on both sides of US 87. Look for color variations including whites, tans, grays, and subtle banding patterns indicating agate or chalcedony. The translucent to opaque quality and waxy luster are key identifiers. Small specimens 1-3 inches across are most common, though occasional larger nodules occur. Use a magnifying glass to examine fragments for internal structure before collecting.

meta_title: Road Cut on US 87 Rockhounding - Texas
meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and quartz at this easy roadside rock cut near Johnson City in Gillespie County. No permit required. Beginner-friendly Texas rockhounding site.
